title: Protection of Audit Information — Tamper Detection on Audit Indexes

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 AU-9 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AU-9 (Protection of audit information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.8: Protection of Audit Information — Tamper Detection on Audit Indexes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.8' running on index=_audit (delete, edit, permissions changes), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=_audit earliest=-24h
| search object="*savedsearches*" OR object="*indexes*" OR object="*authorize*"
| stats values(action) as actions, values(user) as users, count by object
| sort - count
description: Monitors `_audit` for destructive actions against searches, indexes, and roles that could undermine AU-9 protection of audit information.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure `_audit` is indexed and retained per policy; (2) restrict who can delete `_audit`; (3) forward `_audit` to immutable store if required; (4) integrate alerts with SOC for Splunk admin changes. Indexes required: `index=_audit`.
visualization: Table (object, actions), Timeline (admin changes), Single value (critical changes per day)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for monitors `_audit` for destructive actions against searches, indexes, and roles that could undermine protection of audit information. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_audit` (delete, edit, permissions changes).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ensure `_audit` is indexed and retained per policy; (2) restrict who can delete `_audit`; (3) forward `_audit` to immutable store if required; (4) integrate alerts with SOC for Splunk admin changes.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_audit earliest=-24h
| search object="*savedsearches*" OR object="*indexes*" OR object="*authorize*"
| stats values(action) as actions, values(user) as users, count by object
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Protection of Audit Information — Tamper Detection on Audit Indexes (AU-9)** — Monitors `_audit` for destructive actions against searches, indexes, and roles that could undermine AU-9 protection of audit information.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_audit` (delete, edit, permissions changes). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _audit.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_audit, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by object** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (object, actions), Timeline (admin changes), Single value (critical changes per day)
